past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 9 years ago, well controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with oral agents
family_history: Father died of lung cancer at age 71
Mother alive at 85 with hypertension and mild cognitive impairment
Brother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 58, treated successfully
No family history of brain tumors or neurological disorders

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit 12 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.occupation: High school history teacher, currently still working
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home
social_history.exercise: Walks 30 minutes daily, occasional golf on weekends

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time but appears mildly confused and slow to respond. Attention is impaired on serial sevens, completing only two subtractions before losing track. Language is notable for mild word-finding difficulty with occasional paraphasic errors. He can follow two-step commands but struggles with three-step commands. Short-term recall is 1 out of 3 objects at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Visual fields reveal a left homonymous hemianopia on confrontation testing. Extraocular movements are full without nystagmus. Mild left central facial droop. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. Left upper extremity: 4/5 grip strength, 4-/5 deltoid, 4/5 biceps and triceps. Left lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5/5 knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Mild left pronator drift present.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased light touch and pinprick sensation on the left arm and hand. Proprioception intact in bilateral great toes. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+, right plantar flexor. Left upper extremity 3+, left lower extremity 3+ with extensor plantar response (positive Babinski on the left).
neurological_exam.coordination: Right finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact. Left finger-to-nose shows mild dysmetria. Left heel-to-shin mildly impaired.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates with mild left-sided limp and circumduction of the left leg. Mildly wide-based. Unable to tandem walk.
neurological_exam.additional: Papilledema noted bilaterally on fundoscopic examination, more prominent on the right. No meningismus. No asterixis.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headache, left arm weakness, and a witnessed seizure
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Gerald Patterson is a 61-year-old right-handed man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home this morning. His wife reports that over the past three to four weeks, he has been complaining of progressively worsening headaches, predominantly on the right side, that are worst in the early morning and sometimes wake him from sleep. She notes that the headaches are unlike any he has experienced before and are associated with nausea and occasional vomiting. Over the past two weeks, she has also noticed that he has been clumsier with his left hand, dropping things more frequently, and that his left arm seems weaker than usual. He has also had increasing difficulty finding the right words during conversations, which is very uncharacteristic for him as a teacher. His colleagues at work have remarked that he seems less organized and occasionally forgetful over the past month. This morning at approximately 7:15 AM, while eating breakfast, he suddenly stopped speaking, his eyes deviated to the right, and he developed rhythmic jerking of the left arm that rapidly generalized to a full tonic-clonic convulsion lasting approximately 90 seconds. He was postictal and confused for about 20 minutes before EMS arrived. He had never had a seizure before. In the emergency department, a non-contrast CT of the head was obtained and demonstrated a large right frontal mass with surrounding edema and approximately 8 mm of left-to-right midline shift. He was started on levetiracetam and dexamethasone. He has been admitted to the neurosurgery service for further workup and management.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha, attenuated over the right hemisphere
background.symmetry: Significant amplitude asymmetry with attenuation and slowing over the right frontotemporal region
background.reactivity: Present but diminished on the right

findings.morphology: Irregular, high amplitude polymorphic delta waves
findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with structural lesion in the right frontal lobe
May indicate cortical irritability in peri-lesional cortex

findings.morphology: Sharp transients with aftergoing slow wave, phase reversing at F4
findings.clinical_correlation: Epileptogenic potential in peri-tumoral cortex; correlates with the patient's clinical seizure

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to concern for elevated intracranial pressure

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG showing continuous right frontal polymorphic delta activity consistent with a structural lesion, with occasional right frontal sharp waves indicating epileptogenic potential. Background asymmetry with attenuation of the right hemisphere. These findings are consistent with a right frontal space-occupying lesion with associated cortical irritability.
eeg.limitations: Muscle artifact intermittently obscures temporal leads.
recommended_actions: MRI brain with contrast for further characterization of the structural lesion
Continue anti-seizure medication given epileptiform discharges
Consider continuous EEG monitoring if clinical concern for subclinical seizures

findings.type: Heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis
findings.location: Right frontal lobe, centered in the superior and middle frontal gyri, extending to the precentral gyrus posteriorly and the corpus callosum medially

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous hypointensity with central areas of further hypointensity consistent with necrosis
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous hyperintensity with central T2-bright necrotic cavity
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive surrounding hyperintensity representing vasogenic edema extending into the right parietal white matter and corona radiata
signal_characteristics.T1_post_gadolinium: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with nodular enhancing components along the medial and posterior walls
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim indicating high cellularity; central necrotic cavity shows no restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Low ADC values at the enhancing margin confirming restricted diffusion from hypercellularity

findings.mass_effect: Approximately 8 mm left-to-right midline shift at the level of the septum pellucidum. Compression of the right lateral ventricle with early trapping. Effacement of the right-sided cortical sulci. No uncal or tonsillar herniation.
findings.borders: Irregular, infiltrative margins with finger-like projections of FLAIR signal extending along white matter tracts

findings.location: Right frontal and parietal white matter, extending along the corona radiata and into the posterior limb of the right internal capsule

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the white matter

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to midline shift and ventricular compression

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified elsewhere in the brain
Mild chronic microvascular ischemic changes in the periventricular white matter bilaterally
No abnormal signal in the cervical cord on available sagittal images
Bilateral carotid and vertebral arteries appear patent on MRA sequences
mri.impression: 1. Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontal mass (5.8 x 4.3 x 4.1 cm) with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim, extensive surrounding vasogenic edema, and 8 mm of midline shift. Imaging features are highly characteristic of a high-grade glioma, most likely glioblastoma (WHO grade IV). 2. Mass effect with compression of the right lateral ventricle but no frank herniation. 3. No evidence of multifocal disease or leptomeningeal spread on this study. Neurosurgical consultation for tissue diagnosis and management is recommended.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Thick irregular ring enhancement, central necrosis, infiltrative margins, restricted diffusion at enhancing rim, solitary lesion in frontal lobe
Usually multiple, more spherical, located at gray-white junction; solitary metastasis possible but less likely without known primary
Typically homogeneous enhancement without necrosis, periventricular location, more common in immunocompromised patients
Smooth thin ring enhancement, central restricted diffusion (pus), patient is afebrile with normal WBC

recommended_actions: Neurosurgical consultation for maximal safe resection
MRI perfusion and MR spectroscopy for further characterization
Stereotactic biopsy if complete resection not feasible
Pre-surgical functional MRI for motor and language mapping given proximity to precentral gyrus

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes and acute stress response

Other.clinical_significance: Upper limit of normal; can be mildly elevated in high-grade gliomas
Consistent with known type 2 diabetes, suboptimally controlled

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are largely within normal limits. Glucose is mildly elevated consistent with known diabetes and acute physiological stress from seizure. HbA1c of 7.1% indicates suboptimally controlled diabetes. LDH is at the upper limit of normal. Coagulation parameters, hepatic function, and renal function are normal, establishing a safe baseline for potential surgical intervention and chemotherapy. CBC is unremarkable with no evidence of infection or hematologic abnormality.
abnormal_values_summary: Glucose 142 mg/dL (elevated, consistent with diabetes and stress)

findings.location: Enhancing rim of the right frontal mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Maximum rCBV ratio of 5.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter (markedly elevated)
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Elevated regional cerebral blood flow corresponding to the enhancing tumor rim
signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Elevated permeability in the enhancing regions consistent with blood-brain barrier disruption

findings.location: Central necrotic cavity of the right frontal mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Very low rCBV in the central necrotic region, as expected

additional_observations: Hot spot analysis demonstrates the highest rCBV along the posterolateral enhancing rim
No elevated perfusion in the surrounding FLAIR-bright edema, suggesting vasogenic edema rather than infiltrative tumor in this region
output.impression: MRI perfusion demonstrates markedly elevated rCBV (ratio 5.8) at the enhancing rim of the right frontal mass, strongly supporting the diagnosis of a high-grade glioma. The elevated cerebral blood volume reflects tumor neoangiogenesis. The central necrotic component shows expected low perfusion. These perfusion characteristics are classic for glioblastoma and help distinguish this from lower-grade gliomas (typically rCBV < 1.75) or non-neoplastic etiologies.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: rCBV ratio > 4 at enhancing rim is strongly associated with WHO grade IV glioma

recommended_actions: Proceed with surgical planning for maximal safe resection
Consider intraoperative MRI or 5-ALA fluorescence guidance

findings.type: Abnormal metabolite ratios in enhancing tumor
findings.location: Right frontal enhancing mass, solid component

signal_characteristics.choline: Markedly elevated choline peak (Cho/Cr ratio = 4.2, Cho/NAA ratio = 6.8)
signal_characteristics.NAA: Markedly reduced N-acetylaspartate peak, consistent with neuronal loss/displacement
signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks at 1.3 ppm, indicating necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

additional_observations: Spectroscopic pattern in the peritumoral FLAIR-hyperintense region shows mildly elevated Cho/Cr ratio (1.6), suggesting possible tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margin
Contralateral normal-appearing white matter shows normal metabolite ratios
output.impression: MR spectroscopy demonstrates markedly elevated choline (Cho/Cr = 4.2), markedly reduced NAA, and prominent lipid/lactate peaks within the enhancing tumor component. This metabolite profile is characteristic of a high-grade glioma with high cellularity, membrane turnover, and necrosis. The mild choline elevation in the peritumoral region raises concern for infiltrative tumor extending beyond the enhancing margin.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Markedly elevated Cho/Cr > 3, lipid/lactate peaks, very low NAA
Abscess would show amino acid peaks (valine, leucine) and succinate rather than elevated choline

recommended_actions: Consider extended resection margins guided by spectroscopy findings

CT_Staging.value: No pulmonary masses or nodules. No lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal. No pleural effusions.
CT_Staging.clinical_significance: No evidence of primary lung malignancy

CT_Staging.value: Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenals, and kidneys unremarkable. No focal hepatic lesions. No abdominal lymphadenopathy. Mild prostatic enlargement consistent with BPH.
CT_Staging.clinical_significance: No evidence of primary abdominal malignancy

CT_Staging.value: No pelvic masses. No pathologic lymphadenopathy. Mild prostatic enlargement.

output.interpretation: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrates no evidence of a primary extracranial malignancy. This effectively rules out brain metastasis from a common primary site (lung, colon, kidney, melanoma) and supports the diagnosis of a primary brain tumor. Incidental mild prostatic enlargement is consistent with the patient's known BPH.

Histopathology.value: High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis
Histopathology.clinical_significance: Histologic features diagnostic of glioblastoma, WHO grade IV
Markedly elevated proliferative index, consistent with high-grade malignancy

Molecular_Markers.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; associated with poorer prognosis compared to IDH-mutant gliomas
Unmethylated MGMT promoter predicts reduced benefit from temozolomide chemotherapy
Absence of 1p/19q co-deletion excludes oligodendroglial lineage; consistent with astrocytic tumor
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma and may be relevant for targeted therapy trials
TERT promoter mutation supports the diagnosis of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma per WHO 2021 classification

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy confirms the diagnosis of glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV (WHO 2021 classification). The tumor demonstrates classic histologic features including microvascular proliferation and palisading necrosis. Molecular profiling reveals IDH-wildtype status, EGFR amplification, TERT promoter mutation, and unmethylated MGMT promoter. The unmethylated MGMT status suggests reduced benefit from temozolomide, though standard Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide and radiation followed by adjuvant temozolomide) remains the standard of care. EGFR amplification may qualify the patient for clinical trials of targeted therapies.
abnormal_values_summary: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV confirmed

findings.location: Left precentral gyrus (primary motor cortex for right hand)

signal_characteristics.BOLD_activation: Robust activation in the left precentral gyrus during right hand motor task, normally located

findings.type: Motor cortex activation with tumor proximity
findings.location: Right precentral gyrus (primary motor cortex for left hand)

signal_characteristics.BOLD_activation: Activation present in the right precentral gyrus during left hand motor task, but displaced posteriorly by the tumor mass. Activation focus is approximately 8 mm from the posterior enhancing margin of the tumor.
signal_characteristics.laterality_index: Right hemisphere motor activation present but partially displaced

findings.mass_effect: Tumor displaces the right precentral gyrus posteriorly

findings.location: Left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca area) and left superior temporal gyrus (Wernicke area)

signal_characteristics.BOLD_activation: Strong left-lateralized language activation in both Broca and Wernicke areas during verb generation and sentence comprehension tasks

additional_observations: The right-sided motor cortex activation for left hand is close to the posterior tumor margin (approximately 8 mm separation), which places it at risk during resection
Language areas are in the contralateral (left) hemisphere and not at risk from right frontal surgery
DTI tractography shows the right corticospinal tract is displaced medially and posteriorly by the tumor but remains intact
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates left-lateralized language dominance with no involvement of the right frontal tumor in critical language networks. Motor mapping shows the right precentral gyrus (controlling the left hand and arm) is displaced posteriorly by the tumor, with activation approximately 8 mm from the enhancing tumor margin. This proximity necessitates careful intraoperative motor mapping and cortical/subcortical stimulation during resection to preserve motor function. DTI tractography confirms the right corticospinal tract is displaced but intact.
recommended_actions: Awake craniotomy or intraoperative cortical/subcortical motor mapping to protect the displaced right motor cortex
Consider intraoperative DTI tractography guidance to protect the corticospinal tract

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during radiation, then 150-200 mg/m2 days 1-5 of 28-day cycle
interactions: Metformin: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction, but temozolomide-related nausea may affect oral medication absorption
contraindications: Absolute neutrophil count must be >= 1500/uL and platelet count >= 100,000/uL before each cycle
Hepatic function must be monitored given concurrent statin use
warnings: Temozolomide causes myelosuppression; CBC must be monitored weekly during concurrent radiation and before each adjuvant cycle

Risk of opportunistic infections including Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia during concurrent radiation; prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole recommended, but patient has sulfa allergy — consider pentamidine inhalation or dapsone as alternatives
Hyperglycemia may worsen with concurrent dexamethasone use; close glucose monitoring and insulin adjustment likely needed
alternatives: Tumor Treating Fields (TTFields/Optune) as adjunctive therapy

output.query: glioblastoma standard treatment protocol Stupp temozolomide radiation

results.title: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.summary: Landmark RCT demonstrating that adding temozolomide to radiotherapy improved median survival from 12.1 to 14.6 months and 2-year survival from 10% to 27% in glioblastoma patients.

results.title: Effects of radiotherapy with concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide versus radiotherapy alone on survival in glioblastoma: long-term follow-up of the EORTC/NCIC trial
results.authors: Stupp R, Hegi ME, Mason WP, et al.
results.summary: Long-term follow-up of the Stupp trial showing 5-year overall survival of 9.8% with temozolomide vs 1.9% with radiotherapy alone. MGMT methylation was the strongest predictor of benefit.

results.title: MGMT gene silencing and benefit from temozolomide in glioblastoma
results.authors: Hegi ME, Diserens AC, Gorlia T, et al.
results.summary: MGMT promoter methylation status predicts benefit from temozolomide. Methylated patients had significantly better outcomes. Unmethylated patients derived less benefit but the Stupp protocol remains standard of care.

output.summary: The standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma is the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent temozolomide (75 mg/m2 daily) with focal radiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks), then adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2 for 5 days every 28 days, 6-12 cycles). MGMT promoter methylation is the strongest molecular predictor of temozolomide response. For MGMT-unmethylated tumors, the protocol remains standard, though benefit is reduced. Tumor Treating Fields (TTFields) can be added to adjuvant temozolomide based on the EF-14 trial. For elderly patients (>70 years), abbreviated radiation with or without temozolomide is an option.

differential.key_features: Usually multiple lesions at gray-white junction with known primary cancer; CT staging negative for extracranial primary in this case
Typically homogeneously enhancing, periventricular, more common in immunocompromised patients; this patient is immunocompetent and the lesion shows ring enhancement with necrosis
Would expect smooth thin ring enhancement, central restricted diffusion from pus, fever, elevated WBC; patient is afebrile with normal WBC and restricted diffusion is at the rim not center
Open ring enhancement pattern, less mass effect relative to size, younger patient demographic, history of MS; patient is older male with thick complete ring enhancement

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: administer IV levetiracetam 1000 mg for seizure prophylaxis and dexamethasone 10 mg IV to reduce cerebral edema
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant interactions with current medications; note sulfa allergy for future PCP prophylaxis planning

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast including DWI sequences
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontal mass with central necrosis, ring enhancement, surrounding vasogenic edema, and midline shift — classic glioblastoma appearance

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizure activity and characterize epileptogenic focus
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right frontal continuous polymorphic delta activity with occasional sharp waves, consistent with structural lesion and epileptogenic potential

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, hepatic panel, and LDH as pre-surgical baseline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal labs establishing safe surgical baseline; mildly elevated glucose from diabetes and stress

optimal_actions.action: Request CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out extracranial primary malignancy (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of extracranial malignancy, supporting primary brain tumor diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Request MRI perfusion study to characterize tumor vascularity and grade
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV at enhancing rim (ratio 5.8), supporting high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy for metabolite analysis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peak — classic high-grade glioma metabolite profile

optimal_actions.action: Request functional MRI for pre-surgical motor and language mapping given tumor proximity to motor cortex
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right motor cortex displaced posteriorly, 8 mm from tumor margin; left-lateralized language — motor mapping critical for surgical planning

optimal_actions.action: Refer to neurosurgery for maximal safe resection with intraoperative motor mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical planning incorporating fMRI and DTI data for safe resection

optimal_actions.action: After tissue diagnosis, initiate Stupp protocol: concurrent temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily with focal radiotherapy 60 Gy in 30 fractions, followed by adjuvant temozolomide
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard of care post-operative treatment for glioblastoma

critical_actions: Administer anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam) immediately after presenting seizure
Administer dexamethasone to reduce cerebral edema and mass effect
Obtain MRI brain with gadolinium contrast for definitive lesion characterization
Obtain tissue diagnosis through biopsy or resection with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q)
Perform metastasis workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) to exclude secondary brain tumor
Refer to neurosurgery for maximal safe resection
Plan for Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide and radiation) after surgical resection
contraindicated_actions: Lumbar puncture — contraindicated due to large mass with significant midline shift and risk of uncal herniation
Administering high-dose anticoagulation without clear indication — risk of intratumoral hemorrhage
Starting steroids without concurrent PPI or H2 blocker for GI prophylaxis
Delaying surgical evaluation in the setting of significant mass effect and midline shift
Withholding anti-seizure medication after a first seizure with a structural lesion
Whole brain radiation as initial treatment — focal radiation per Stupp protocol is standard for newly diagnosed GBM
key_reasoning_points: New-onset seizure in a 61-year-old man with progressive headache and focal neurological deficits (left arm weakness, left homonymous hemianopia) demands urgent neuroimaging to rule out a structural lesion
Morning headaches with nausea/vomiting and papilledema indicate elevated intracranial pressure from mass effect
CT showing a large right frontal mass with midline shift is highly suggestive of a primary brain tumor in a patient without known systemic cancer
MRI features of thick irregular ring enhancement, central necrosis, restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim, and infiltrative margins are classic for glioblastoma
Cross-modal consistency: right frontal lesion on MRI correlates with left-sided motor deficits, left homonymous hemianopia, and right frontal EEG findings
Metastasis workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) is essential to distinguish primary brain tumor from metastatic disease
Proximity of tumor to right precentral gyrus makes functional MRI and intraoperative mapping critical to preserve motor function
Molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q) is essential for definitive WHO 2021 classification and treatment planning
MGMT unmethylated status predicts reduced temozolomide benefit but does not change the standard Stupp protocol recommendation
Sulfa allergy must be considered when planning PCP prophylaxis during concurrent temozolomide and radiation

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generation System
key_teaching_points: Classic glioblastoma presentation with progressive headache, focal deficits, and seizure
Importance of comprehensive imaging including perfusion, spectroscopy, and functional MRI
WHO 2021 classification requires molecular profiling for definitive glioma diagnosis
Stupp protocol remains standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma regardless of MGMT status
